71.def:
Definition

Essential (Immediate) Newborn Care (ENC): A set of evidence-based, cost-effective interventions — drying, warming, cord care, airway management, early breastfeeding, Vitamin K, and birth vaccines — delivered in the first hour of life (the “Golden Hour”) to prevent hypothermia, infection, haemorrhage, and neonatal death.
71.conclusion:
Conclusion

Immediate newborn care is the nurse-midwife’s most critical responsibility at every delivery. Following the DWCABFV sequence — Dry, Warm, Cord, Airway, Breathe, Feed, Vitamins/Vaccines — within the Golden Hour prevents the majority of neonatal deaths. Under the NHM & INC mandate, every nurse must be competent in ENC and apply it consistently at the PHC, FRU, and district hospital level.

72.def:
Definition

Neonatal Assessment: A systematic, head-to-toe clinical evaluation of the newborn performed at birth (APGAR scoring), within 2 hours, within 24 hours, and before discharge — to confirm normality, detect congenital anomalies, and initiate early intervention where required.
72.conclusion:
Conclusion

Systematic neonatal assessment is a core nursing competency mandated by the INC curriculum and the NHM Home-Based Newborn Care (HBNC) programme. The nurse-midwife is often the first and only clinician to examine the newborn; early detection of danger signs — absent red reflex, imperforate anus, abnormal tone — and prompt referral can be life-saving. Timely, complete documentation of each assessment finding is equally essential.

73.def:
Definition

APGAR Score (Virginia Apgar, 1952): A standardised 10-point scoring system that evaluates five physiological parameters — Appearance (colour), Pulse (heart rate), Grimace (reflex irritability), Activity (muscle tone), and Respiration — at 1 minute and 5 minutes after birth to assess neonatal transition and guide resuscitation decisions.
73.conclusion:
Conclusion

The APGAR score is a rapid, universally adopted tool that guides the nurse-midwife in deciding whether a newborn needs stimulation, oxygen, or full resuscitation. The 5-minute score is more predictive of neonatal outcome than the 1-minute score. Critically, under NRP (Neonatal Resuscitation Programme) guidelines, resuscitation must begin immediately on clinical assessment — never delayed while awaiting the APGAR score. Accurate recording in the delivery register fulfils both MUHS documentation and medicolegal requirements.

74.def:
Definition

Caput Succedaneum: Diffuse oedematous swelling of the scalp soft tissue, crossing suture lines, caused by pressure of the birth canal; present at birth and resolves in 2–3 days.
Cephalhaematoma: Collection of blood between the skull bone and its periosteum (subperiosteal haematoma), confined within one skull bone (does not cross suture lines); appears hours after birth and resolves over 6–8 weeks.
74.conclusion:
Conclusion

Distinguishing caput succedaneum from cephalhaematoma is an essential competency for the nurse-midwife conducting routine newborn examination. The key differentiating feature is suture-line crossing: caput crosses, cephalhaematoma does not. Cephalhaematoma carries a risk of hyperbilirubinaemia as the blood reabsorbs, requiring monitoring for jaundice. Reassuring parents and documenting the finding accurately are important aspects of the nurse’s role under INC guidelines.

75.intro:
Introduction

Fontanels are important anatomical landmarks in the newborn skull that serve both functional and clinical roles. In India, where a large proportion of deliveries occur at primary health centres and homes, the nurse-midwife must be able to palpate fontanels correctly and interpret findings such as bulging or sunken soft spots — signs that can indicate serious conditions requiring urgent referral.
75.conclusion:
Conclusion

The fontanels are a valuable clinical window that the nurse uses at every well-baby visit and sick-newborn assessment. A bulging fontanel signals raised intracranial pressure (meningitis, hydrocephalus) requiring emergency referral, while a sunken fontanel signals dehydration requiring urgent rehydration. Under the INC & MUHS curriculum, knowledge of fontanel anatomy and clinical significance is a core competency of neonatal nursing care.

76.intro:
Introduction

Immunization is one of the most cost-effective public health interventions available, preventing an estimated 2–3 million child deaths worldwide every year. In India, the Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) — one of the world’s largest — targets all children from birth to 5 years and pregnant women, with vaccines delivered free of charge at government health facilities. The nurse-midwife is the primary implementer of this programme at the community level.
76.conclusion:
Conclusion

Immunization is a fundamental nursing responsibility under the National Health Mission (NHM) and the INC scope of practice. The nurse-midwife must maintain the cold chain, adhere to the UIP schedule, document vaccines on the mother–child protection (MCP) card, manage minor adverse reactions, and counsel families against vaccine hesitancy. Early and complete immunization of every child is the nurse’s contribution to India’s goal of eliminating vaccine-preventable diseases.

77.intro:
Introduction

The Baby Friendly Hospital Initiative (BFHI) was launched jointly by WHO and UNICEF in 1991 to reverse the global decline in breastfeeding caused by hospital practices that promoted formula feeding. In India, over 70% of mothers initiate breastfeeding, yet exclusive breastfeeding rates remain low; BFHI-accredited facilities play a critical role in supporting mothers to breastfeed correctly from the first hour of birth, reducing infant mortality and malnutrition.
77.conclusion:
Conclusion

BFHI accreditation reflects a hospital’s commitment to evidence-based breastfeeding support, guided by the Ten Steps to Successful Breastfeeding. The nurse-midwife is the cornerstone of BFHI implementation — educating mothers antenatally, initiating breastfeeding within one hour of birth, demonstrating correct positioning & attachment, and prohibiting pre-lacteal feeds. Under the INC mandate and NHM RMNCH+A strategy, promoting exclusive breastfeeding for six months is a priority nursing action that directly reduces infant morbidity and mortality in India.
